7 JULY 1960

I. THE COMMUNIST BLOC

Congo Republic--Mutiny within Force Publique jeopardizes security

text:
Leopoldville vicinity.

Somali Republic as yet unable to form government because of sectional rivalry and tribal difficulties.

Japanese leftists plan demonstration at US air base (Atsugi) on 10 July.

III. THE WEST

West Berlin coalition government's stability may be shaken by political activity of Mayor Brandt.

West Germany protests Khrushchev's abuse of Austrian hospitality in attacking Adenauer; top Austrian officials regard protest as support of their objections to Chancellor Raab's association with Khrushchev.

Paraguay--Pressures to oust Stroessner dictatorship growing

text:
New Somali Republic Still Without a Government

The Somali Republic, harassed with sectional rivalry and tribal difficulties, has been unable to form a government since achieving independence on 1 July. Announcement of the formal unification of Somalia and former British Somaliland, which became independent on 26 June, has been delayed because negotiators from each territory insist on naming the prime minister and are unable to reach agreement over the allocation of ministerial portfolios.

Representatives from the Somaliland region of the republic, having supported the election of Aden Abdullah Osman--former president of Somalia's Legislative Assembly--for chief of state, now insist on naming the prime minister and at least three of the other ten ministers envisaged in the new government. If these demands are not met, they may return to Hargeisa without ratifying the Act of Union.

Somalis from the eastern (Mogadiscio) region, however, seek to retain the office of prime minister for one of their own members, although they are unable to agree on a candidate. Former Prime Minister Abdullahi Issa, a member of the Hawiya tribal group who was considered the front-runner from the governing Somali Youth League (SYL), is encountering considerable opposition from the popular Abdirascid Ali Scermarche, the leading Darot tribal member in the old Legislative Assembly

text:
Paraguayan Revolutionary Pressures

A group of prominent Paraguayans, including some clergymen, military officials, and student leaders, gave President Stroessner a memorandum on 22 June urging him to turn over power to a transitional junta composed of representatives of the political parties and the armed forces.

The junta would guarantee Stroessner's personal safety to remain
token_text: paraguayan revolutionary pressures a group of prominent paraguayans including some clergymen military officials and student leaders gave president stroessner a memorandum on 22 june urging him to turn over power to a transitional junta composed of representatives of the political parties and the armed forces the junta would guarantee stroessner's personal safety to remain

text:
or leave the country and would hold elections at the end of the year. The group planned to wait for a short while, and if no reply were forthcoming, to begin a "campaign of resistance."

Presentation of the memorandum is another reflection of the increasing pressures on the Stroessner dictatorship and offers a possibility of avoiding the bloodshed threatened by the four principal groups which have recently stepped up their revolutionary activities against the regime. These activities have thus far featured small guerrilla action inside Paraguay and small invasions from border points, but there are reports that the rebels plan large-scale fighting in the near future.

Stroessner's military support now is strong but would not be deaf to alternative propositions in the face of a strong revolutionary tide
